coredata.dc:title: Interfacial modification layers based on carbon dots for efficient inverted polymer solar cells exceeding 10% power conversion efficiency
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Xiaofeng
Yang, Yuzhao
Nian, Li
Su, Hua
Ou, Jiemei
Yuan, Zhongke
Xie, Fangyan
Hong, Wei
Yu, Dingshan
Zhang, Mingqiu
Ma, Yuguang
Chen, Xudong
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we have demonstrated that rationally-designed carbon dots (C-dots) with amino groups, for the first time, were used as an efficient interfacial modification layer on the ZnO or AZO interlayers, greatly improving the device performance. The C-dots modifying showed decreased work function and smoothed surface of metal oxides, facilitating the enhancement of charge extraction efficiency and the decrease of recombination losses for the cathode. More importantly, the C-dots showed the luminescent down-shifting effect, beneficial for the increase of the light conversion for near ultraviolet and blue-violet portions of sunlight. As a result, by incorporating ZnO/C-dots as the interlayers, our designed inverted polymer solar cells with PTB7:PC71BM as the active layer obtained over 20% enhancement compared with the devices with pure ZnO (from 7.41% to 9.01%). Notably, a maximum PCE of up to 10.24% was achieved with the AZO/C-dots as the interlayers and PTB7-Th:PC71BM instead of PTB7:PC71BM as the active layer, and this efficiency outperforms all previously reported PSCs using carbon materials as additives in the active layer or the interface materials. Our PCE value is also higher than those of many previously reported bulk-heterojunction (BHJ) solar cells using the organic polymer modifier.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Interfacial modification layers
Carbon dots
Inverted polymer solar cells
Power conversion efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Al doped zinc oxide (AZO)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Shing Foundation and the Ministry of Education of China
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51233008 , 51503228 ) and the Youth 1000 Talent Program of China . Appendix A

Supplementary Figure S1 Supplementary material . Xiaofeng Lin received his B.S. degree in Applied Chemistry from Sun Yat-Sen University (SYSU, China) in 2013. He is currently a Ph.D. student from Professor Xudong Chen's group in Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education at SYSU. His research is focused on the optical spectroscopy and device characterization of polymer solar cells. Yuzhao Yang received her B.S. degree from Sun Yat-Sen University (SYSU, China) in 2009 and Ph.D. degree from SYSU in 2014. Now she is a postdoctor in Professor Xudong Chen's group in Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education at SYSU. Her research is focused on the optical spectroscopy and device engineering of polymer solar cells. Li Nian received his B.S. degree and M.S. degree in Chemistry from Jilin University (China) in 2010 and 2013, respectively. He is now a Ph.D. student at South China University of Technology (SCUT, China). His research is focused on the design and synthesis of interfacial materials for organic solar cells. Hua Su received his B.Eng. degree from Southwest University of Science and Technology (China) in 2009. In the same year, he joined Prof. Zong-Wan Mao's group in Sun Yat-Sen University, and worked on his pH. D. thesis of synthesis of inorganic-organic hybrid nanomaterials for catalysis and biomedical applications. He obtained his Ph.D. degree in 2015. Now he is a research assistant of Prof. Wei Wang's group in State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science in Nanjing University. His current research interest is focused on singe nanoparticle catalysis study based on single molecule fluorescence technology. Jiemei Ou received her B.S. degree and Ph.D. degree from School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Sun Yat-Sen University (SYSU, China) in 2010 and 2015 . Now she is a postdoctor in Professor Xudong Chen's group in Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education at SYSU. Her current research thesis focuses on the design and synthesis of new type SERS-MEF dual mode nanoprobes for biodetections and bioimaging using conjugated polymers. Zhongke Yuan obtained his B.S. degree in Material Chemistry at South West University in 2012. He is currently a Ph.D. student under the supervision of Prof. Xudong Chen at Sun Yat-sen University. His research is centered on the synthesis and applications of polymer composites. Fangyan Xie received her Ph.D. degree in Condensed State Physics in 2005 from Sun Yat-Sen University, and joined Instrumental Analysis & Research Center of Sun Yat-Sen University in 2005. In 2011 and 2014 she was a visiting scholar in Chinese University of Hong Kong and University of Rochester respectively. Dr. Xie's research interest includes, electronic structures of the organic devices, degradation mechanism of polymer solar cells using photo-emission spectroscopy. Wei Hong received his Ph.D. in material science in 2014 from Shanghai Jiaotong University (China). He joined School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University (China) as a lecturer in 2014. Dr. Hong's research interest includes photonic crystals, plasmonic nanomaterials, and their application in photoelectric devices. Dingshan Yu received his Ph.D. In Optical Engineering in 2008 from Sun Yat-Sen University (China). After post doctoral training at University of Dayton (USA), Case Western Reserve University (USA), and Nanyang Technological University (Singapore) for more than six years, he joined Sun Yat-Sen University as a professor in 2015. Dr. Yu's research interest includes design and synthesis of polymeric functional composites and carbon materials as well as their application in optoelectronic devices and energy storage and conversion system. Mingqiu Zhang is a professor in Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education, Sun Yat-Sen University (SYSU, China). He has over thirty years of systematic experience in polymers, polymer blends and polymer composites. He serves as a member of the Asian-Australasian Association for Composite Materials (AACM) Council and the standing council of Chinese Materials Research Society. In 2005, the Li Ka Shing Foundation and the Ministry of Education of China selected him as a Cheung Kong Scholar. In addition, Professor Zhang is on the editorial board of eight scientific journals and holds twenty-eight patents. Yuguang Ma is a Professor and Director in State Key Laboratory of Luminescent Materials and Devices, South China University of Technology (SCUT, China). Before joining the SCUT, he was a professor of polymer chemistry at Jilin University. He is Cheung Kong Scholar distinguished professor and received State Natural Science Award (2nd Prize) in 2009. His research interest focuses on the organic and polymer optoelectronic materials and devices. Xudong Chen is a professor in Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education, Sun Yat-Sen University (SYSU, China). His research interest includes design and synthesis of polymeric functional composites, organic/polymer optoelectronic devices and the application of plasmonic nanomaterials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the inverted polymer solar cell modified with a C-dots layer. Top left – the chemical structures of PTB7 or PTB7-Th and PC71BM; Top right – illustration of the synthesis of the C-dots; Bottom right – the TEM image of C-dots.
J-V characteristics of the inverted polymer solar cells (Glass/ITO/interlayer/PTB7:PC71BM/MoO3/Al) (a) under illumination (100mWcm−2) and (b) in dark with corresponding interlayers.

SEM images of (a) ZnO, (b) ZnO/C-dots, (c) AZO and (d) AZO/C-dots. AFM images of (e) ZnO, (f) ZnO/C-dots, (g) AZO and (h) AZO/C-dots. (i) The UPS spectra of the film samples (Glass/ITO/interlayer) with various interlayers. (j) Illustration of vacuum level shift and reduced WF of the film samples (Glass/ITO/interlayer) after deposition of the C-dots layer.

(a) J-V characteristics of the electron-only devices (Glass/ITO/interlayer/PTB7:PC71BM/Ca/Al) with corresponding interlayers. (b) The EQE spectra of the inverted polymer solar cells (Glass/ITO/interlayer/PTB7:PC71BM/MoO3/Al) with corresponding interlayers.
Summary of electrical parameters for the inverted polymer solar cells with corresponding interlayers and active layers.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The electrical parameters of inverted polymer solar cells. The device structure is Glass/ITO/interlayer/active layer/MoO3/Al.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of various parameters for the inverted polymer solar cells with corresponding interlayers.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Surface root mean square roughness (R q) of the film samples (Glass/ITO/interlayer) was measured by AFM.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: WF of the film samples (Glass/ITO/interlayer) was measured by UPS.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Electron mobility of Electron-only devices (ITO/interlayer/PTB7:PC71BM/Ca/Al) was measured using the method of SCLC.

head.ce:title: Interfacial modification layers based on carbon dots for efficient inverted polymer solar cells exceeding 10% power conversion efficiency

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education of China, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510275, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education of China, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Polymer Optoelectronic Materials and Devices, State Key Laboratory of Luminescent Materials and Devices, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Polymer Optoelectronic Materials and Devices, State Key Laboratory of Luminescent Materials and Devices, South China University of Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: X. F. Lin and Y. Z. Yang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we have demonstrated that rationally-designed carbon dots (C-dots) with amino groups, for the first time, were used as an efficient interfacial modification layer on the ZnO or AZO interlayers, greatly improving the device performance. The C-dots modifying showed decreased work function and smoothed surface of metal oxides, facilitating the enhancement of charge extraction efficiency and the decrease of recombination losses for the cathode. More importantly, the C-dots showed the luminescent down-shifting effect, beneficial for the increase of the light conversion for near ultraviolet and blue-violet portions of sunlight. As a result, by incorporating ZnO/C-dots as the interlayers, our designed inverted polymer solar cells with PTB7:PC71BM as the active layer obtained over 20% enhancement compared with the devices with pure ZnO (from 7.41% to 9.01%). Notably, a maximum PCE of up to 10.24% was achieved with the AZO/C-dots as the interlayers and PTB7-Th:PC71BM instead of PTB7:PC71BM as the active layer, and this efficiency outperforms all previously reported PSCs using carbon materials as additives in the active layer or the interface materials. Our PCE value is also higher than those of many previously reported bulk-heterojunction (BHJ) solar cells using the organic polymer modifier.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer solar cells (PSCs) based on conjugated polymers and fullerene composites have been developed rapidly during the past decades because of their various advantages, such as low-cost processing and suitability to industrial manufacturing of large-area and flexible devices [1]. Particularly, inverted polymer solar cells (i-PSCs) as the counterpart of the conventional polymer solar cells (c-PSCs) show major advantages of increased stability as well as enhanced device performance. The rapid advance of power conversion efficiency (PCE) benefits from the development of novel materials with improved photovoltaic properties [2,3], optimizing fabrication processing such as implementing smart architectures [4], controlling morphology [5], and inserting of interlayers to modify electrode [6,7]. In particular, the cathode interlayer between the active layer and conductive ITO electrode plays a key role for the performance of i-PSCs due to their fundamental importance for the electron extraction process.

As for the cathode interlayer used in i-PSCs, zinc oxide (ZnO) and Al doped ZnO (AZO) are promising candidates due to their high electron mobility, excellent optical transparency and solution-based fabrication [8]. However, there is a mismatch between conduction band energy level of ZnO or AZO and the LUMO level of PC71BM [9]. Moreover, as typical inorganic materials, ZnO or AZO has poor interfacial contact with organic active layers, which may lead to large series resistance (Rs), weak electronic coupling and severe back charge recombination [10]. An effective approach to solve this problem is to modify the metal oxide surface with an organic interlayer, especially polyelectrolytes with amino groups, which optimized the work function (WF) of the interlayers as well as the contacts between the interlayers and the active layers. Woo et al. demonstrated that using poly(ethyleneimine) (PEI) to modify the surface of ZnO, significant improvement in PCE can be achieved from 6.99% to 8.76% for the polymer thieno[3,4-b]thiophene/benzodithiophene (PTB7):[6,6]-phenyl-C71-butyric acid methyl ester (PC71BM) pair [11]. Heeger's group demonstrated that PCE was increased by 25% for organic small molecule solar cells by incorporating polyethylenimine ethoxylated (PEIE) atop ZnO [12]. These fascinating results show a bright future of modifying metal oxide surface with suitable organic materials to achieve co-interlayers for highly efficient i-PSCs. However, these organic polymer modifiers suffered from the insulating properties and the lack of effective utilization of the incident light, consequently limiting the device efficiency. Thus, it is highly desirable to develop new types of interface modifier to replace conventional polymer modifier for high-performance photovoltaic device applications.

Recently, a variety of carbon materials such as graphene oxide (GO) [13,14], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [15] and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [16], have been used as either the additive in the active layer or electron/hole extraction materials to improve the PCE of the PSCs. Among them, 0-dimensional carbon dots (C-dots) are particularly attractive for photoelectronic device applications due to excellent properties such as wavelength-dependent luminescence emission [17], good water-soluble processability [18], tunable optical and electronic properties [19], and photochemical stability [20]. For example, owing to the luminescent down-shifting effect of C-dots, C-dots can be used as the additive in the active layer and luminescent down shifting layer in PSCs [21–23], showing greatly enhanced device performance. However, to our knowledge, C-dots have not been explored as interfacial modification layer in PSCs.

In this work, we demonstrate that water-soluble carbon dots (C-dots) with amino groups can be used as excellent interface modifier materials for the first time atop metal oxide interlayers to greatly enhance the performance of PSCs. Our proposed C-dots modification atop the ZnO or AZO interlayer could overcome the above-mentioned limitation of the existing polyelectrolyte-based modifier including the insulating nature and the lack of utilization of the incident light owing to the good electrical conductivity and luminescent down-shifting effect associated with the C-dots, while the luminescent down-shifting effect of C-dots could increase the light conversion of near ultraviolet and blue-violet portions of sunlight. More importantly, the C-dots modification can result in decreased work function and smoothed surface of the metal oxides, thus enhancing charge extraction efficiency and reducing recombination losses for the cathode. As expected, by incorporating ZnO/C-dots as the interlayers, our designed inverted polymer solar cells with PTB7:PC71BM as the active layer obtained over 20% enhancement (relative to the devices with pure ZnO, from 7.41% to 9.01%). Notably, a maximum PCE of up to 10.24% was achieved with the AZO/C-dots as the interlayers and polymer poly[[2,6′-4,8-di(5-ethylhexylphenyl)benzo[1,2-b;3,3-b]dithio-phene][3-fluoro-2[(2-ethylhexyl)carbonyl]thieno[3,4-b]thiophenediyl]] (PTB7-Th):PC71BM [24–26] instead of PTB7:PC71BM as the active layer, and our PCE outperforms all previously reported PSCs using carbon materials as additives in the active layer or the interface materials and is also higher than those of many previously reported bulk-heterojunction (BHJ) solar cells using the organic polymer modifier.

PTB7 (average MW=80,000–200,000, PDI≤3.0), PC71BM (purity>99%), and 1,8-diiodoctane (DIO) (purity>98%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (United States). PTB7-Th (PCE-10, Polymer:OSO100, Lot # YY8226) was purchased from 1-Material (Canada). Two kinds of active solutions, consisted of PTB7:PC71BM and PTB7-Th:PC71BM respectively (the blended ratios of polymer:PC71BM was 1:1.5 by weight), were prepared using the mixed-solvent of chlorobenzene (CB) and 1,8-diiodoctane (DIO) (CB:DIO=97:3 by volume).

Zinc acetate dihydrate (0.985g) was dissolved in methanol (42mL) at 60°C, a solution of KOH (0.49g) in methanol (22mL) was added to the zinc acetate dihydrate solution under vigorous stirring for 2.5h. Then the product was treated with acetone (15mL) to dissolve the nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Al doped zinc oxide (AZO)
ce:section.ce:para: Zn(OAc)2·2H2O (1.0g), Al(OH)(OAc)2 (0.015g, (1at%)) and Zonyl FSO-100 (0.03g) were mixed in deionized water (10mL) as described by Stubhan [28]. The mixture was stirred for 2h and filtered through a 0.45µm filter to remove insoluble materials.

The synthesis of C-dots was based on the method of Yang, Song and Wei's work [29–31]. Firstly, different amounts of 4,7,10-Trioxa-1,13-tridecane- diamino (TTDDA) and glucose were added to distilled water to form a transparent solution. Then the solution was heated in a 500W microwave over for 10min. Within a short time, the solution changed from colorless to yellow and finally to dark brown. The dark brown color implies the formation of C-dots.

The ZnO or AZO layer (thickness=ca. 30nm) was deposited on the cleaned ITO-glass substrates at 2500rpm for 30s by spin-coating the ZnO or AZO precursor solutions. Then, the ZnO and AZO layer-coated substrates were baked at 200°C for 5min and 150°C for 10min in air, respectively. After cooling, the C-dots (5mg/mL, hydrosolvent) was spin-coated on the surface of the ZnO or AZO layer at 2500rpm for 30s (ca. 10nm), followed by thermal annealing at 140°C for 10min. Next, the active layer (ca. 180nm) was spin coated on top of the C-dots-coated ZnO or AZO layer and dried inside a vacuum glove box for 3h to remove DIO. Finally, MoO3 (10nm) and Al (80nm) were sequentially deposited on the active layer through a shadow mask by thermal evaporation in a vacuum of 1×10−6 Torr, which defines the active area (0.16cm2) and form a top anode.

The current density versus voltage (J-V) characteristics were measured by a Keithley 2400 source meter. The PCE values were measured under 1sun, AM 1.5G spectrum from a solar simulator (Japan, SAN-EI, XES-40S1), the 100mWcm−2 solar simulator illumination intensity was calibrated by a monocrystal reference silicon cell (PV Measurements, with KG-5 visible filter), and the silicon cell was calibrated by National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL). Spectral mismatch factors (M) of 1.03 was calculated according to a standard procedure was used to obtain the correct photocurrent and efficiency for the PTB7 devices as published previously [32,33]. PL spectra were taken using an FLS920 combined time-resolved and steady state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The EQE was measured by a QE measurement system (PV measurements, Inc.) to confirm the J sc values.

In a typical experiment, C-dots with amino groups (–NH2) were synthesized by microwave method similar to previously reported procedure [29] (top right of Fig. 1, and the details of synthetic methods and mechanism in Section 2 and Supplementary Fig. S1, respectively). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observation of C-dots (Fig. 1, bottom right) indicates that the average diameter of individual C-dot is ca. 8nm. The fourier transform infrared (Supplementary Fig. S2), X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS, Supplementary Fig. S3) and elemental analysis (EA, Table S1) results indicate that the C-dots were successfully passivated by 4,7,10-Trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA). Also, X-ray diffraction pattern of C-dots (Supplementary Fig. S4) showed a broad peak located at 23.40°, indicating the amorphous nature of C-dots [29]. In addition, by using the linear potential scans, the LUMO and HOMO values of C-dots are determined to be −2.80eV and −5.75eV, and the energy gap of C-dots is calculated to be about 2.95eV (details in Supplementary Fig. S5). Herein, the C-dots are, for the first time, used as an interfacial modification layer (thickness=ca. 10nm, by cross-section SEM, show in Supplementary Fig. S7; solid resistivity≤600Ωcm, show in Supplementary Fig. S8) to improve device performance of the i-PSCs (Glass/ITO/interlayer (ZnO or AZO)/PTB7:PC71BM /MoO3/Al). The schematic structures of the reference device without C-dots and the C-dots incorporated device are also shown in Fig. 1 (bottom left).

The current density versus voltage (J-V) characteristics of the i-PSCs under AM 1.5G irradiation at 1000W/m2 and in the dark are shown in Fig. 2, while the corresponding open-circuit voltage (V oc), short circuit-current density (J sc), fill factor (FF) and power-conversion efficiency (PCE) are listed in Table 1. Clearly, after incorporating C-dots as the surface modifier of ZnO or AZO, the open-circuit voltage, short-circuit current and fill factor for both ZnO and AZO devices show simultaneous increase (Table 1). For instance, relative to the reference AZO device, its counterpart device with C-dots exhibits an increased V oc (0.75V vs 0.73V), an enhanced J sc (17.65mAcm−2 vs 16.19mAcm−2) and an improved FF (69.50% vs 67.43%), hence achieving a much higher PCE (9.20% vs 7.96%). It is noted that the devices used AZO as the interlayer in present study showed better performance relative to those used ZnO as the interlayer with the same device architecture. This is, most probably, because of better conductivity of AZO than ZnO [28,34]. Meanwhile, we also tested a series of devices with various concentration of C-dots and active layers and finally achieved a optimal PCE of 10.24% (details in Tables 1 and S4 and Supplementary Figs. S9 and S10) from the C-dots (0.5wt%) devices based on PTB7-Th:PC71BM instead of PTB7:PC71BM, which is one of the best results for single-junction PSCs. Our PCE outperforms previously reported PSCs using carbon nano-materials as either additive in the active layer or electron/hole extraction layer, such as adding carbon-dot-supported Ag nanoparticle into hole extraction layer (8.31%) [21], blending GQDs into the active layer (7.60%) [35] and utilizing GO as electron-transport layer (7.50%) [36]. To our knowledge, this is the highest value among all reported polymer solar cells incorporated with carbon materials so far (Tables S2 and S3). Our efficiency value is also higher than those of many previously reported BHJ solar cells using the organic polymer modifier like PEI (8.76%) [11], PEIE (7.88%) [12] and PFN (8.5%) [37]. Abundant measurements were carried out to clarify the enhanced mechanism of C-dots as the interfacial modification layer as discussed below.

Firstly, field emission-scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM) were used to understand the effects of C-dots on the surface defects of ZnO or AZO interlayers as shown in Fig. 3. In the absence of C-dots, FE-SEM observation of the ZnO and AZO film samples (Fig. 3(a) and (c)) show that the average size of the ZnO or AZO nanoparticle is ca. 30–40nm. Although the surface features of the C-dots film were limited by the resolution of FE-SEM (Fig. 3(b) and (d)), the differences between the film samples without and with C-dots can be identified by analyzing the surface roughness of the samples. AFM was used to determine the roughness of film samples' surface without (Fig. 3(e) and (g)) and with (Fig. 3(f) and (h)) C-dots. By modifying ZnO or AZO with C-dots, the root mean square (RMS) roughness reduced from 2.25nm (ZnO) to 1.39nm (ZnO/C-dots) and from 2.59nm (AZO) to 1.97nm (AZO/C-dots), respectively ( Table 2). The result indicates that the surface morphology of C-dots samples is much smoother. The smoother surface could enhance the physical contact [11] between the metal oxide/C-dots layer and the active layer, leading to the improved shunt resistance (R SH) (584Ωcm2 (ZnO) vs 820Ωcm2 (ZnO/C-dots), 638Ωcm2 (AZO) vs 770Ωcm2 (AZO/C-dots), see in Table 2). The increased R SH is consistent with the reduced reverse current in the dark J-V characteristics (Fig. 2(b)), which benefits for the increase of FF values [32,33].

Next, ultraviolet photo-electron spectroscopy (UPS) was used to probe the electronic properties of the cathodes including ZnO, ZnO/C-dots, AZO and AZO/C-dots modified ITO. After applying C-dots onto ZnO or AZO layer, the WF (Fig. 3(h)) decreased from −3.89eV (ZnO) to −3.50eV (ZnO/C-dots) and −3.80eV (AZO) to −3.39eV (AZO/C-dots), respectively (Table 2). The C-dots made a vacuum level shift of about 0.4eV (Fig. 3(j)), resulting in a significant decrease in the WF of ZnO or AZO. This is attributed to the characteristics of interface dipole created by C-dots with a mass of amino groups [38,39] on the surface of the C-dots, which was confirmed by XPS (Figs. S11 and S12, details in Supplementary Information). The data is consistent with the increased V oc values from 0.73V (i-PSCs based on ZnO or AZO interlayer) to 0.75V (i-PSCs based on ZnO/C-dots and AZO/C-dots). Moreover, the decreased WF value of C-dots modified ZnO or AZO allows for the formation of ohmic contacts [21] with PC71BM acceptor and thus results in the increase of the built-in field, which is advantageous for enhancing charge extraction efficiency and reducing recombination losses [40,41].

To understand the effect of C-dots modifying on the electron transport properties of the interlayers, electron-only devices were fabricated with device configurations: ITO/interlayer/PTB7:PC71BM/Ca/Al ( Fig. 4(a), Supplementary Fig. S13). The electron mobility of the devices based on ZnO or AZO interlayer were 1.53×10−4 cm2/(vs) and 1.69×10−4 cm2/(vs), respectively. After the surface modification of C-dots, the electron mobility increased to 3.16×10−4 cm2/(vs) and 3.63×10−4 cm2/(vs) for ZnO/C-dots and AZO/C-dots, respectively (Table 2). Compared with the hole-only devices based on PTB7:PC71BM with the hole mobility of 3.2×10−4 cm2/(vs) [39], the enhancement of electron mobility after the C-dots modification is beneficial for the carrier balance. The increase in electron mobility is attributed to the improved film morphology and the enhanced physical contact [42,43] between the metal oxide/C-dots layer and the active layer.

Finally, external quantum efficiency (EQE) and steady state photoluminescence (PL) were measured to analyze the enhancement of J sc. EQE spectra of various devices are shown in Figs. 4(b) and S14. The EQE curves of the devices with the C-dots as interfacial modification layer are intensified in the wavelength range between 340 and 500nm, which comes from the combination effect of the interfacial modification (bettter contact, fast electron transport and reduced work function) and the luminescent down-shifting effect of C-dots [12,22,23,44,45]. The PL spectra (Supplementary Fig. S15) indicate that C-dots have a broad emission under irradiation of near ultraviolet portions (340–500nm), and the emission peaks are located in the region of 460–560nm. The fluorescence quantum yield (PLQY) of C-dots was estimated to be about 3.3% (Supplementary methods). Therefore, after incorporating C-dots into a PSC, more incident sunlight can be effectively utilized and converted to the light due to effective light conversion of near ultraviolet and blue-violet portions of sunlight, which is so-called the luminescent down-shift effect [22,23,44]. Furthermore, in the region of 500–800nm, the EQE curves in this part have slight enhancement because of the interfacial modification (bettter contact, fast electron transport and reduced work function) [12,45]. The EQE values approach about 84% at around 685nm for the PSCs with the C-dots. The J sc values calculated by integrating the EQE spectra from 300nm to 800nm are 15.19mAcm−2 (ZnO), 15.89mAcm−2 (AZO), 16.91mAcm−2 (ZnO/C-dots) and 17.23mAcm−2 (AZO/C-dots), respectively, which are in good agreement with J sc obtained from J-V curve (less than 5% error, Supplementary Table S5).

In conclusion, we have demonstrated the first use of carbon dots (C-dots) with amino groups as an excellent interfacial modification layer atop the ZnO or AZO interlayer for high-performance inverted polymer solar cell (i-PSCs). The C-dots modification can not only decrease the work function and roughness of the metal oxide interlayer, but also facilitate the transport and collection of the photo-generated charge carriers, hence leading to the enhancement of V oc and FF. Moreover, the C-dots show the luminescent down-shifting effect, thus leading to the enhancement in the J sc values. As a result, an optimized inverted polymer solar cell using AZO/C-dots as the interlayer and the PTB7-Th:PC71BM as active layer achieve a maximum PCE of up to 10.24%, outperforming the reference devices without C-dots and previously reported carbon nano-material incorporated PSCs, even many BHJ solar cells using the polymer-based interface modifier. We highlight that combining ZnO and the novel amorphous C-dots as cathode interlayer can achieve high efficiency in i-PSCs, which makes the C-dots replace the existing organic polymer interface modifier for high performance device applications. Therefore, our present study opens new avenues for development of new class of interface materials for high performance solar cells and many other optoelectronic device applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51233008, 51503228) and the Youth 1000 Talent Program of China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.nanoen.2016.05.011.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary Figure S1
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaofeng Lin received his B.S. degree in Applied Chemistry from Sun Yat-Sen University (SYSU, China) in 2013. He is currently a Ph.D. student from Professor Xudong Chen's group in Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education at SYSU. His research is focused on the optical spectroscopy and device characterization of polymer solar cells.

Yuzhao Yang received her B.S. degree from Sun Yat-Sen University (SYSU, China) in 2009 and Ph.D. degree from SYSU in 2014. Now she is a postdoctor in Professor Xudong Chen's group in Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education at SYSU. Her research is focused on the optical spectroscopy and device engineering of polymer solar cells.

Li Nian received his B.S. degree and M.S. degree in Chemistry from Jilin University (China) in 2010 and 2013, respectively. He is now a Ph.D. student at South China University of Technology (SCUT, China). His research is focused on the design and synthesis of interfacial materials for organic solar cells.

Hua Su received his B.Eng. degree from Southwest University of Science and Technology (China) in 2009. In the same year, he joined Prof. Zong-Wan Mao's group in Sun Yat-Sen University, and worked on his pH. D. thesis of synthesis of inorganic-organic hybrid nanomaterials for catalysis and biomedical applications. He obtained his Ph.D. degree in 2015. Now he is a research assistant of Prof. Wei Wang's group in State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science in Nanjing University. His current research interest is focused on singe nanoparticle catalysis study based on single molecule fluorescence technology.

Jiemei Ou received her B.S. degree and Ph.D. degree from School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Sun Yat-Sen University (SYSU, China) in 2010 and 2015 . Now she is a postdoctor in Professor Xudong Chen's group in Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education at SYSU. Her current research thesis focuses on the design and synthesis of new type SERS-MEF dual mode nanoprobes for biodetections and bioimaging using conjugated polymers.

Zhongke Yuan obtained his B.S. degree in Material Chemistry at South West University in 2012. He is currently a Ph.D. student under the supervision of Prof. Xudong Chen at Sun Yat-sen University. His research is centered on the synthesis and applications of polymer composites.

Fangyan Xie received her Ph.D. degree in Condensed State Physics in 2005 from Sun Yat-Sen University, and joined Instrumental Analysis & Research Center of Sun Yat-Sen University in 2005. In 2011 and 2014 she was a visiting scholar in Chinese University of Hong Kong and University of Rochester respectively. Dr. Xie's research interest includes, electronic structures of the organic devices, degradation mechanism of polymer solar cells using photo-emission spectroscopy.

Wei Hong received his Ph.D. in material science in 2014 from Shanghai Jiaotong University (China). He joined School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University (China) as a lecturer in 2014. Dr. Hong's research interest includes photonic crystals, plasmonic nanomaterials, and their application in photoelectric devices.

Dingshan Yu received his Ph.D. In Optical Engineering in 2008 from Sun Yat-Sen University (China). After post doctoral training at University of Dayton (USA), Case Western Reserve University (USA), and Nanyang Technological University (Singapore) for more than six years, he joined Sun Yat-Sen University as a professor in 2015. Dr. Yu's research interest includes design and synthesis of polymeric functional composites and carbon materials as well as their application in optoelectronic devices and energy storage and conversion system.

Mingqiu Zhang is a professor in Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education, Sun Yat-Sen University (SYSU, China). He has over thirty years of systematic experience in polymers, polymer blends and polymer composites. He serves as a member of the Asian-Australasian Association for Composite Materials (AACM) Council and the standing council of Chinese Materials Research Society. In 2005, the Li Ka Shing Foundation and the Ministry of Education of China selected him as a Cheung Kong Scholar. In addition, Professor Zhang is on the editorial board of eight scientific journals and holds twenty-eight patents.

Yuguang Ma is a Professor and Director in State Key Laboratory of Luminescent Materials and Devices, South China University of Technology (SCUT, China). Before joining the SCUT, he was a professor of polymer chemistry at Jilin University. He is Cheung Kong Scholar distinguished professor and received State Natural Science Award (2nd Prize) in 2009. His research interest focuses on the organic and polymer optoelectronic materials and devices.

Xudong Chen is a professor in Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education, Sun Yat-Sen University (SYSU, China). His research interest includes design and synthesis of polymeric functional composites, organic/polymer optoelectronic devices and the application of plasmonic nanomaterials.
